title: [Long-term Warming Induces a Shift in Microbial Nutrient Limitation in Alpine Meadow Soils].
author_string: Huang, De-Shuai, Liu, Ji, Wang, Cong, Zhou, Jia-Cong, Cui, Yong-Xing et al.
journal_full: Huan jing ke xue= Huanjing kexue
abstract: To reveal the effects of long-term warming on soil extracellular enzyme activity, nutrient stoichiometry, and microbial nutrient limitation in the alpine meadow ecosystem on the Tibetan Plateau, this study explores the response mechanisms of microbial nutrient limitation at different soil depths under climate warming. A 13-year simulated warming experiment was conducted at the Haibei Grassland Ecosystem Station, located in the Haibei Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture. In the study, soil extracellular enzyme activities [β-glucosidase （BG）, cellobiohydrolase （CBH）, N-acetyl-β-glucosaminidase （NAG）, leucine aminopeptidase （LAP）, and alkaline phosphatase （AP）] and nutrient stoichiometry at soil depths of 0-10 cm and 10-30 cm were observed. Using vector length and vector angle, coupled with soil physicochemical properties, the impact of warming treatment on microbial carbon （C）, nitrogen （N）, and phosphorus （P） limitations in different soil layers was analyzed. The results indicated that, compared to those in the control, long-term warming did not significantly alter the C∶N ratio or C∶P ratio of extracellular enzymes in the 0-10 cm soil layer, but these ratios decreased significantly by 41.25% and 24.14%, respectively, in the 10-30 cm soil layer. The N∶P ratio of extracellular enzymes significantly increased by 36.00% and 30.14% in the 0-10 cm and 10-30 cm layers, respectively. Warming promoted the accumulation of soil organic carbon （SOC） in the 0-10 cm layer, alleviating microbial C limitation. The vector lengths in the 0-10 cm and 10-30 cm soil layers decreased by 5.31% and 34.34%, respectively, indicating that the alleviation of C limitation was more pronounced in the 10-30 cm layer than in the 0-10 cm layer. Warming shifted the vector angle in the 0-10 cm layer from 45.60° to 37.56°, indicating that microbes shifted from P limitation to N limitation. In the 10-30 cm layer, the vector angle remained above 45° after warming, suggesting that microbial P limitation was significantly reduced but not completely replaced by N limitation. Furthermore, the decline in AP activity reduced the availability of P, increasing microbial reliance on N. Random forest analysis revealed that SOC and total N content were the primary predictors of microbial N and P limitation. The findings of this study highlight the profound effects of long-term warming on nutrient stoichiometry and microbial metabolic adaptation mechanisms in alpine meadow ecosystems. Microbial communities dynamically regulated extracellular enzyme secretion to optimize nutrient acquisition and achieve metabolic balance across different soil layers. These results provide scientific evidence for predicting nutrient dynamics in alpine grassland ecosystems under climate warming and offer theoretical support for the protection and management of these ecosystems.
